label: help_request

text: lol, no, you don’t have to pay it. It sounds like the account was likely made under her name and social so if it goes unpaid it goes to collections against her credit. What’s going to be hard is checking 2 things: if you can generate a number transfer pin(manage tab-settings-permissions and controls-transfer pin- create pin), and to ensure you don’t have port protection on under add ons and line level services). If you can get the number transfer pin, ensure you don’t have port protection on, and get the account number, take the two numbers plus your phone number to a prepaid service like mint mobile, and get it transferred for now so it’s not a risk of your mom canceling your number and you lose it. Once your number is moved off she will no longer have control or access to it, and then you can shop around for the carrier you want to stay with
label: deal_or_policy_info

text: So your mom is the account holder and she basically owns your phone number and your sisters phone number. There are 3 ways to get off her plan and go onto your own. Option 1-Change of ownership, she can release your phone number and you accept it and go onto your own plan, assuming your staying with T-Mobile, if there is a payment balance remaining on your phone, have them transfer that as well. Option 2-You leave T-Mobile and go to a different carrier. This one is pretty straight forward. You will need the T-Mobile account number and will need to generate a transfer pin number. Only your mom can generate the transfer pin number being its her account. Also make sure port protection under your number is turned off. Option 3-You go on your own plan and get a new phone number. There are cheap prepaid options starting as low as $15 a month. The only problem is I would suggest getting a new phone as well if you plan on doing that. The reason is your current phone is still attached to her account and she can report the phone lost or stolen and it can blacklist your phone basically making it a paperweight. You buy unlocked phones directly from Apple/Samsung/Google/Best Buy or go on eBay/Swappa for unlocked phones too. Also the T-Mobile account cannot be suspended if you do plan on switching carriers or staying with T-Mobile and keeping your phone number.
label: deal_or_policy_info

text: Considered switching until experiencing customer service Currently on Verizon with 2 lines. We are going camping in an area with no cell service, so I wanted to add 2 standalone T-Satellite lines. For this, T-Mobile assigns you a separate eSIM/phone number for T-Satellite only that runs alongside your existing carrier on devices that support multiple active eSIMs. Line 1 (Android): Signed up online (name, email, existing phone number, phone IMEI) in about 5-10 minutes. Went to phone settings and added the new eSIM right away. Once the line was activated, I used my newly assigned T-Mobile phone number to create an account for login and management. No issues. Line 2 (iPhone): Logged into my new T-Mobile account and tried to add a second T-Satellite. After entering all the details, the system prompted me to contact customer service. After a few minutes on the T-Life app navigating unhelpful AI chatbots and requesting a human, a rep told me I cannot add another T-Satellite only line to my account because it needs to refresh, which takes a week. They clarified that if I wanted another line now, I would need to create a whole new account. This makes no sense, but I need the line before the trip, so I proceed. Signed up online for a T-Satellite only line again under a new account for Line 2 using my wife s details (name, email, existing phone number, phone IMEI, etc.). Received email with a confirmation number and link to eSIM activation instructions, but no account number, phone number, or eSIM QR code. Unlike Line 1, the notification to add the new eSIM never arrived. Back on T-Life chat, I provided all the details (confirmation number, iPhone make/model, IMEI). The new rep asks me to repeat everything I already typed in the initial message, then tells me to call T-Mobile support. Get on the phone with T-Mobile support. They struggle to verify my account because I can t provide an account ID or phone number (not in the confirmation email) and can t create an account online without the phone number (which I don t have yet because the eSIM never activated). A circular problem no one on the phone can solve. 30 minutes later, I got transferred to technical support and had to re-explain everything. After more hold time, they tell me to Google search images of T-Mobile eSIM QR codes and scan it on the iPhone. Having more expertise than the supposed technical support, I inform them that won t work because eSIM QR codes are personalized, not generalized. Still tried it anyway and confirmed it didn t work. An hour and 20 minutes into the call, they tell me to just go to a T-Mobile store for assistance. Every layer of the support experience failed. The online signup email gives you nothing useful when something goes wrong and it s clear T-Mobile support doesn t have the tools or training to fix it either. If the experience is this bad just trying to add a line, how much worse is it for everything else? Anyone else run into similar issues? submitted by

text: I hit that point in 2015. Was a RSM in corporate, had worked with TMO for 8 years (retail for 15). At that point, I had survived the SunCom merger and then the death of the AT T merger. I know - totally different company between now and then. But I felt the burnout. I had just finished a year in a store that I took over from a shady shady shady RSM with crazy inflated numbers. He of course was promoted, and I had customers out the door pissed that they had contracts and lines they didn’t want, and an unachievable activation goal because of all of the fraud. My final straw was my DM requiring us to do the same shady shit another store did - all new lines came with “a free month of unlimited data and PHP”. The expectation was that we credited customers’ bills for that first month, and call them on day 31 to ask if they still wanted it. If not, we would remove / downgrade. Day 31 meant we would still get commission chargebacks, but it wouldn’t reverse off of reporting. And we had 100% premium data and PHP attach rates. Alas. I worked my network and had a RAM that I had fired a year before. Well - helped her “realize it was time to move on”, she was pissed. But clearly got over it because she landed a job for a software company and needed to hire a new sales executive, and she thought I would be perfect for it… so I went to work for her for a few years while I finished my accounting degree. Total M-F office job making calls all day. I finished my degree a couple of years later, got a job in public accounting, and then landed a decent job working remotely for a client that I audited in accounting. Have been there 6 years now and am comfortable. Today is Friday- and that actually means something. Alas. That’s just my random story. Just saying that I absolutely remember feeling that way - stuck. You didn’t get into your current position without making a network. Get to working it - your ticket to the next place in life might be someone you fired a year ago 🙃.
label: employee_complaint

text: Left me speechless. T-Mobile service is a joke here’s what I’ve dealt with Ever since switching to T-Mobile, my service has been terrible. Constantly dropped calls, SIM failures, and losing service completely. And the best part? The GPS is so inaccurate it tells me I’m on the opposite side of the street and completely freaks out it just doesn’t work. I’ve called T-Mobile over 16 times. Half the reps say they’re “working on towers,” while the other half say everything looks fine and that I’m “all purple” on their coverage map so I shouldn’t be having any issues. Finally, after what felt like the 17th call, they told me my phones were outdated and I needed to upgrade. Fine—I was due for an upgrade anyway. I got the new phones, hoping it would fix everything. Nope. Out of the box? 1 bar. Constantly switching between 5G and EDGE. I thought maybe it just needed time to settle in. A few weeks later? Somehow it got worse. They told me to “enable Wi-Fi calling.” I have Xfinity gigabit internet my home network should be rock solid. But even then, when I walk around my house, calls drop, it switches to E, and the service is still crap despite having full Wi-Fi bars. I called them one last time and said either lower my bill or give me a decent credit, because this is insane. Their response? “You’re on our top plan, you shouldn’t be having these issues. But we’ll try one more thing: delete your voicemails, and it should fix everything.” What? Then they added, “If that doesn’t work, we’ll credit your account for the inconvenience and lower your monthly bill.” Okay, sure. Let’s try this for the 52,296th time. A week later—no improvement, same garbage service. They call me the next Monday and ask, “Any better?” Me: “Nope, not at all. Where’s my credit?” And this is exactly what the rep said: “Since we gave you a late fee credit last month, we can only give you a $10 credit this time and we can’t reduce your bill.” I’m paying nearly $300/month for 3 lines with phone financing. I said, “Ma’am, this can’t be right. I’m paying this much and you’re offering me $10? That doesn’t even scratch the surface.” I mentioned that I might be better off switching to Cricket or Mint Mobile—I’d probably get better service for way less money. Her response? “Well, if you don’t want the credit, we can port your numbers out right now.” What the actual fuck? She literally gave me an ultimatum Here’s $10. Enjoy our shitty service, or let me help you cancel. Edit. Even the insurance company they work with is terrible. text: I have a major bone to pick with T-Mobile I ve been a customer since 2012. In all that time, I ve never missed a payment, never had my service disconnected, and never had any issues with my account. Recently, I had to take unpaid medical leave from work because my young daughter is hospitalized with complications related to cancer treatment. I went back to work last week and just needed a little flexibility on my $160 bill while I got caught up financially. I called T-Mobile and they offered me a payment arrangement: half now, half two weeks later. I actually told them I could pay the first half immediately and the second half within two weeks. For whatever reason, they wouldn t let me make the payment right then without fees, so they scheduled it for the next day. Unfortunately, one of my daughter s medical bills hit my account two days earlier than expected, so the T-Mobile payment bounced. I called back immediately and explained what happened. I asked if I could just go back to the original arrangement they had already offered me. Their answer? No. Because I had defaulted on the first arrangement, they would no longer offer any payment arrangements at all. My only option was to pay the entire balance in full. I couldn t do that at the time. Yesterday I picked up some side work and got paid in advance. My plan was to put what I could toward the bill and catch up. Then today my service gets shut off. Not only that, but my bill magically jumps from $160 to $235. I lost my $15 AutoPay discount and now they re charging me a $60 reconnect fee. Here s the part that really got me: when I called today after my service was disconnected, the automated system immediately offered me another payment arrangement. Pay part on the 13th, pay the rest two weeks later, and they ll restore service right away. So let me get this straight. Less than 24 hours after my original arrangement failed because of unexpected medical expenses, I was told absolutely no payment arrangements were available. But once my service is shut off and you ve added another $75 in charges to my bill, suddenly a payment arrangement is no problem? I ve been a customer for 14 years. One rough month. One missed arrangement because my kid s medical expenses came first. And this is how T-Mobile handles it? The $60 reconnect fee alone feels CRIMINAL. They can turn service off with the click of a button and turn it back on the same way. Charging people $60 for that, especially long-time customers going through hardship, feels less like a fee and more like a punishment. Am I overreacting here, or is this as absurd as it feels? I’m going to call them tomorrow to see if I can get that $60 waived. This is BS! submitted by
label: help_request

text: A few things here- I do vote with my wallet. So do millions of other people who make a lot more money than I do. These people vote to protect their wallets and only theirs. I don’t have a running balance. The $160 I owe is for one month, I don’t have a huge, running balance. And I’m not sure how you came up with “poor financial literacy” because my kids cancer came back. I am a one-income family, with a good education and a good job. I had just over $8000 in savings two weeks before I went on leave. A third of that went towards a surgery she needed, to which she ran into some complications, which is what led to my leave. I had to continue to pay on her medical bills while out of work, as well as continue to pay just to live. The thing is, most big and small companies have hardship programs for people who run into sudden, emergency situations. I didn’t give background for sympathy- I have never been a “poor me” type of person. I gave background for context. Because I am not a person living paycheck to paycheck, splurging on items that I don’t need, with no savings or backups. My family was hit with a sudden emergency, completely out of my control. I live within my means, I pay my bills, I have good credit and I work hard for everything I have. I get the premise of your point, but you missed the mark on several assumptions (including the fee being valid- when I called they said the $60 was inaccurate and it’s actually only $20, to which they waived). text: The pros: - Schedule flexibility. No weekends/Holidays - WFH depending if you live near an HQ or not - Quota is roughly 65, could be less in SMRA market - Mileage reimbursement is .70 a mile - Pay scale remains in same level as an RSM with 9k RSU The Cons: - Can t prospect businessesswith more than 12 employees. - Building a funnel from scratch means you re probably going to work weekends and nights until you ve established a solid clientele book of business. - No longer have store leads as support. - Less tools than SMB for prospecting. - Pay may not be worth the grind vs RSM pay unless you re really motivated to hustle first 6 months to a year to build that funnel. - Role is relatively new and Pay structure isn t established. - TFB is always on hot seat to get chopped during Reorg and doesn t guarantee you a way back to a leadership role in retail if you leave.
label: employee_complaint

text: T-mobile Cacellation Caught in Neverending Circular Loop of Death Buyer beware if you re trying to cancel your T-Mobile account. I have been trying to completely close my T-Mobile account for nearly six months, and I am STILL being charged. Here s what happened: My wife and daughter were the only active users on the account. When we decided to leave T-Mobile, I successfully ported both of their numbers to another carrier. My own cell phone is provided by my employer and was never an active line on the account, even though I was the account holder (keep this in mind as it comes into play later). After porting out the two active lines, I assumed any remaining charges were simply the final device payments. However, I later discovered that I was being billed for a ghost line that was still active on the account despite us never using it and having no device associated with it. When I called to cancel the account, T-Mobile wanted to verify my identity by sending a one-time PIN. I told them fine and to send it to my phone since I am the account holder. They informed me that the PIN could only be sent to an active line on the account. Well guess what? There are no active lines left on the account! Their solution was for me to visit a store, verify my identity in person, obtain a temporary PIN, and then call customer service back. So I spend another 1 to 2 hours to do exactly that. I spent the time, went through the verification process, requested that the account be completely closed, paid the final balance they told me I owed, and left believing the issue was finally resolved. Fast forward one month. I receive another email stating that a new bill is scheduled to be charged through AutoPay!!! I call again, only to be told that before they can even discuss the account, I have to return to a store and complete the verification process all over again. At this point, I have spent months trying to cancel an account with no active phone lines, made multiple calls, visited a store, paid the balance I was told was due, and yet I am somehow still being billed. Has anyone else experienced this? If so, how did you finally get it resolved? I m seriously considering filing complaints and pursuing small claims court because this has become absolutely ridiculous. Someone please help with this madness. UPDATE: The saga continues... After my original post, I went back to a T-Mobile store yet again and showed my driver s license in person just so I could be verified and speak with a customer service representative about my account. Let that sink in for a moment—I have to physically visit a store and show ID simply to discuss an account that I have been trying to cancel for months. My goal was straightforward: find out why I was still scheduled to be charged through AutoPay despite multiple calls and requests to completely close the account. As usual, I had to explain the entire story from the beginning. After reviewing the account, the representative told me they believed there may have been fraudulent activity involving the mysterious third ghost line that had remained on my account. They recommended opening a fraud investigation and convinced me to leave the account open temporarily while the investigation was completed. The representative explained that if the investigation confirmed the line should not have been there, I might be entitled to credits for the charges I had already paid. Against my better judgment, I agreed. My understanding was that they would complete the investigation quickly and contact me with the results before the next billing cycle so that we could finally resolve everything. About a week later, the representative called me back. Unfortunately, I missed the call. No problem, right? I ll just call back and speak with the representative handling my case. Wrong. The voicemail contained no details, and when I called T-Mobile, I learned that apparently nobody could transfer me back to the representative who actually had the information about my investigation. So once again, I found myself explaining the entire situation from the beginning to a new representative. I asked for a supervisor. And this is where the insanity starts all over again. Before they can discuss the account, they need to verify me. Their verification method? Sending a one-time PIN to an active line on the account. The same account that I ve been trying to close. The same account that effectively has no active phone line available to me. The same issue I have explained repeatedly for months. I again pointed out that I am the account holder. I can provide my account information. I can provide my driver s license. I can verify my identity in multiple ways. They have my phone number and email address. Yet they still refuse to send the verification PIN to me because it can only be sent to an active line on the account. How does a customer verify an account that has no usable active lines left? Apparently, by making another trip to a store and starting the entire process over again. And here s the part that really frustrates me. By agreeing to leave the account open for the fraud investigation, I unknowingly gave T-Mobile enough time to process yet another monthly AutoPay charge on the very line that I have repeatedly requested be cancelled. So now I have no idea what the outcome of the fraud investigation was, I have been billed again, and I have to return to a store once more to show my ID and repeat the same mind-numbing process for what feels like the tenth time. At this point, I m not even sure what s more frustrating—the fact that I m still being charged, or the fact that every attempt to resolve the issue sends me right back to the beginning of the same endless loop. Has anyone successfully escaped this cycle? If so, I d genuinely appreciate any advice because I have completely run out of patience and options. And I thought cancelling my LA fitness membership was hard years ago.....this is next level madness. submitted by
label: help_request

text: Done with T-Mobile So frustrated with T-Mobile right now. I’ve been a loyal customer for 15 years, and I’m finally at the point where I’m considering switching. Back in November 2025, I signed up for T-Mobile Home Internet under a sign-up rebate promotion. In-store, reps told me they would submit everything on my behalf and that I’d receive the digital promo card within 12 weeks. Fast forward to February 2026—after hearing nothing, I contacted support and was told the rebate was never received. It had to be resubmitted, resetting my 12-week clock. By April, I decided to cancel the Home Internet because the connection was completely unstable. Before canceling, I explicitly asked multiple times if I would still receive my promotion. Both customer service and the rebate team assured me I would, even giving me a specific arrival date of May 26. Now it’s June, and after yet another follow-up call, I m suddenly being told the rebate was officially denied because I supposedly didn’t meet the requirements. What makes this worse is that it s a pattern. I recently had to spend three months fighting them just to get promised promotional credits fixed on my iPhone line, too. It feels like every single issue requires dozens of phone calls, escalations, and hours of wasted time just to get T-Mobile to honor what they originally promised. submitted by
label: customer_complaint
